items.q: What was the Elizabethan Religious Settlement of 1559?
items.correct: A deliberate via media — a Protestant doctrine and English-language prayer book combined with some Catholic-style ceremony — designed to maximise conformity.
items.misconception: Elizabeth made England fully Protestant and banned Catholic worship outright.
items.random_wrong: Elizabeth reunited England with the Pope to make peace with Spain.
items.why: The settlement was a compromise designed to be broadly acceptable. Students assume binary Protestant/Catholic divides; Elizabeth deliberately blurred them to keep her realm stable.

items.q: Why did Henry II clash with Thomas Becket?
items.correct: Becket resisted Henry's attempt to bring 'criminous clerks' under royal courts via the Constitutions of Clarendon, defending Church independence from royal jurisdiction.
items.misconception: Henry II ordered Becket's assassination personally.
items.random_wrong: They quarrelled because Becket wanted to be Pope.
items.why: Henry's outburst was rhetorical and he did penance after. The dispute was about jurisdiction over clergy, not personal hatred — students dramatise the murder and miss the constitutional issue.

items.q: What was the theory of the four humours?
items.correct: Disease was caused by imbalance of blood, phlegm, yellow bile and black bile; treatment aimed to rebalance them via bloodletting, purging or diet. It dominated for nearly 2,000 years.
items.misconception: Medieval doctors knew about germs but used humours because they had no medicine.
items.random_wrong: The four humours were invented by Pasteur to explain bacteria.
items.why: Humoral theory was a coherent system from Hippocrates and Galen, not ignorance. Students retroject germ theory backwards, missing why educated people believed it for so long.
It's March 1953. “January 1924” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which battle of 1942-43 marked a decisive Soviet victory?
items.why: Correct answer: Stalingrad. “Moscow” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately how many Soviet citizens died in WWII (in millions)?
items.why: Exact answer: 27. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
